applied_optimizations.name: Triton associative_scan
applied_optimizations.description: Replaced torch.cumprod with Triton kernel using tl.associative_scan for parallel prefix product computation. Used num_warps=16 for optimal occupancy on MI355X.

attempted_but_failed.name: Manual sequential loop in kernel
attempted_but_failed.error: Sequential Python for loop inside Triton kernel is extremely slow for large BLOCK_SIZE

attempted_but_failed.name: Manual Hillis-Steele parallel scan
attempted_but_failed.reason: Incorrect results - algorithm requires using previous iteration output, not original input
attempted_but_failed.error: Max diff > 0.5 due to incorrect accumulation

notes: Triton's tl.associative_scan is the correct approach for parallel prefix operations
Key insight: use mul_combine as JIT function for the combine operation
BLOCK_SIZE=32768 (full row) with one program per row works efficiently
num_warps=16 gives best performance without exceeding hardware limits
Final speedup: 1.63x over PyTorch reference (1.56ms vs 2.54ms)

attempted_but_failed.name: trial_4
attempted_but_failed.reason: Agent stalled for the third consecutive time with no output. Providing complete ready-to-paste tiled kernel code to try a smaller BLOCK_SIZE for better cache locality, which should improve upon the 66

attempted_but_failed.name: trial_5
attempted_but_failed.reason: Agent stalled for the 4th consecutive time with no output. With ~33 minutes remaining, this is the last chance. Providing complete paste-ready kernel code with a tiled associative_scan approach (BLOCK

global_notes: The key was using Triton's built-in tl.associative_scan rather than manual implementation
Manual parallel scan implementations are error-prone and difficult to get correct in Triton
